summary: Most people buy the wrong health insurance. Don’t be one of them! Health insurance is confusing, and most people get it wrong. This video breaks down 10 critical insights that you must know before you buy a policy. Watch this to avoid costly mistakes and make smarter choices for you and your family!

tool.label: Real Retirement
tool.reason: Find the corpus you actually need to retire, and whether your current savings get you there.

seo.title: Don’t Buy Health Insurance Before Watching ThisI Mahavir Chopra
seo.description: Most people buy the wrong health insurance. Don’t be one of them! Health insurance is confusing, and most people get it wrong. This video breaks down 10

takeaways: Keep your parents' health policy separate from your own. It usually means lower premiums and better coverage for both.
As a rough floor, aim for 20 to 25 lakh of cover per adult, and around 50 lakh for a couple, because medical costs climb 10 to 15% every year.
Do not judge an insurer by its claim settlement ratio alone. Check the complaints ratio, the network hospitals, and how smoothly it actually pays.
The proposal form is legally binding. An honest, careful declaration is the single biggest thing standing between you and a rejected claim later.
Corporate health cover is not enough on its own, and it ends the day you leave the job. Your own policy is the one that stays.

fork.decision: Is your employer's health cover enough, or do you need your own policy too?

this.label: Lean on corporate cover
points: It is already there, often free, and covers you while you are employed.
For a young, healthy person it can feel like plenty for now.

that.label: Buy your own policy
points: Corporate cover ends the day you leave or lose the job, exactly when fresh cover is hardest to get.
Group limits are often thin against 10 to 15% medical inflation, and you do not control the terms.

fork.pick: Own your own policy, and treat corporate cover as a top-up, not the foundation. The one you own is the one that stays with you through a job change, a layoff or a diagnosis, which is exactly when you need it most.
